coredata.dc:title: Opto-electronic properties of twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Tiutiunnyk, A.
Duque, C.A.
Caro-Lopera, F.J.
Mora-Ramos, M.E.
Correa, J.D.
coredata.dc:description: The electronic and interband optical properties of vertically coupled stacked graphene quantum dots are investigated using the tight-binding method. Both zigzag and armchair edge configurations are taken into account. In particular, the effect of the geometrical shape (triangular or circle-like) and, most prominently, of the angle of twisting between layers is mainly addressed. The optical response is analyzed from the calculated imaginary part of the dielectric function. It is found that the interband absorption threshold is highly dependent on the dot size and geometry: For armchair triangular bilayer graphene dots the optical gap exhibits a moderate increase for smaller angles of twisting, and the structure behaves as an intermediate to a wide gap semiconductor; whereas zigzag triangular bilayer graphene dots are small gap systems in which the twisting causes the appearance of zero-gap states associated with the variation of HOMO and LUMO states resulting from the breaking of zero-energy degeneracy. In the latter case, it is shown that the low-energy transitions between those states are responsible for the main optical response of the structures which indicates possible applications in the THz optoelectronics. Circular dots are chosen in commensurable configurations and also show stronger low-energy absorption thresholds. A particular feature appearing in this case is the presence of Bravais-Moiré patterns in the two-dimensional probability density distributions for large enough dot radii.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of triangular and circular graphene quantum dots. For triangular quantum dots different edges a) zigzag and b) armchair. A circular shape graphene quantum dot is shown in c). d) A relatiive plane rotation around the center of symmetry in the case of a QD with zigzag side. α is the angle of rotation relative to the initial state. a 1 and a 2 are primitive lattice vectors.

Energy spectrum of bi-layer triangular graphene quantum dot with armchair edge configuration as a function of the size of system (L = na 0), for different values of the angle of relative twisting between the layers.

Energy spectrum of bi-layer triangular graphene quantum dot with zigzag edge configuration as a function of the size of system (L = na 0), for different values of the angle of relative twisting between the layers.

Electron probability density at each atomic position for the first state immediately below the Fermi level (HOMO) in a zigzag triangular bilayer graphene quantum dot with side length equal to 36 time the carbon-carbon bound distance.
The same as in Fig. 4 but for the first state immediately above the Fermi level (LUMO).

The variation of the energy band-gap in bi-layer graphene triangular quantum dots as a function of the angle of relative twisting between the layers, for several dot sizes. a) armchair edge configuration; b) zigzag edge configuration.

(a) Energy gap of a zigzag bilayer triangular graphene quantum dot with 7a
cc side length as a function of the angle of relative layer rotation. (1) to (4) are schematic representations of the relative positions of the graphene bilayers after rotations with the indicated angles.

The variation of the imaginary part of the interband dielectric function (in arbitrary units) as a result of the change in photon energy for armchair stacked triangular bilayer graphene quantum dots. Results for three different dot sizes and twisting angles are presented. Solid lines correspond to x-polarization and dashed lines are for the case of y-polarization of the incident light.

2D density plot of the dielectric function (the same as in Fig. 8) as a function of photon energy (horizontal-scale) and the twisting angle (vertical scale) for armchair-edge triangular graphene quantum dots. Left and right columns correspond to x- and y-polarization respectively. The dot sizes considered are: (a–b) 6a
cc ; (c–d) 9a
cc ; (e–f) 12a
cc ; and (g–h) 15a
c
c . Color scale is uniform for all graphics.
The same as in Fig. 8 but for the case of zigzag-edge stacked bilayer triangular graphene quantum dots.

The same as in Fig. 9 but for the case of zigzag-edge stacked bilayer triangular graphene quantum dots. Color scale for figures (c–h) is the same. For (a,b) peak magnitude it is multiplied by 10. The dot sizes involved are 5a
cc ; (c–d) 7a
c
c ; (e–f) 9a
cc ; and (g–h) 10a
c
c .

Energy spectra of bilayer stacked circular graphene quantum dots for different dot sizes and -commensurable- angles of relative layer twisting. For each pair of indices (m, n) appearing on the horizontal axis, the corresponding twisting angle and dot radius are given by cos
α =

2
+ 4 m n +

respectively, with a 0 representing the graphene lattice constant.
The imaginary part of the dielectric function calculated for stacked bilayer circular graphene quantum dots for different dot sizes and twisting angles defined in the same way as in Fig. 12.

Electron density of probability at each atomic position for the first state below the Fermi level (HOMO), for circular bilayer graphene quantum dots with different angles of relative rotation. Labelling indices are in accordance with the relation commented in the caption of Fig. 12. Moiré patterns for the 2D probability density distribution can be noticed.
The same as in Fig. 14 but for the case of the first state above the Fermi level (LUMO).
The chosen values of the sizes and rotation angles of bilayer circular graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Opto-electronic properties of twisted bilayer graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Alta Investigación, CEDENNA, Universidad de Tarapacá, Casilla 7D, Arica, Chile

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electronic and interband optical properties of vertically coupled stacked graphene quantum dots are investigated using the tight-binding method. Both zigzag and armchair edge configurations are taken into account. In particular, the effect of the geometrical shape (triangular or circle-like) and, most prominently, of the angle of twisting between layers is mainly addressed. The optical response is analyzed from the calculated imaginary part of the dielectric function. It is found that the interband absorption threshold is highly dependent on the dot size and geometry: For armchair triangular bilayer graphene dots the optical gap exhibits a moderate increase for smaller angles of twisting, and the structure behaves as an intermediate to a wide gap semiconductor; whereas zigzag triangular bilayer graphene dots are small gap systems in which the twisting causes the appearance of zero-gap states associated with the variation of HOMO and LUMO states resulting from the breaking of zero-energy degeneracy. In the latter case, it is shown that the low-energy transitions between those states are responsible for the main optical response of the structures which indicates possible applications in the THz optoelectronics. Circular dots are chosen in commensurable configurations and also show stronger low-energy absorption thresholds. A particular feature appearing in this case is the presence of Bravais-Moiré patterns in the two-dimensional probability density distributions for large enough dot radii.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is a planar structure with a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal crystal lattice. In the last two decades, graphene and carbon-based nano-structures have been well studied due to the general interest in their properties, such as: high carrier mobility, conductive and transparent, large specific surface area, wide-band and tunable optical absorption [1–3]. Methods such as mechanical exfoliation, chemical vapor decomposition, thermal decomposition of SiC, reduction of graphite oxide, allow us to create structures with predetermined electronic and optical properties [4]. One of these structures are graphene quantum dots (GQDs), two-dimensional fragments of graphene with sizes smaller than 100 nm, that stand out by their multiple applications in biology, optoelectronic and energy [5–7]. GQDs are synthesized using two main approaches: top-down and bottom-up. In top-down methods GQDs are obtained by exfoliation or decompositions of graphene base nanostructures. On the other hand, in bottom-up methods GQDs are obtained by chemical interaction between small aromatic molecules [5,8].

In the case of GQDs, it is worth to highlight the possibility of tuning their physical-chemical properties by means of changing their geometry, the functionalization of their edges, by including impurities, or by applying external fields [9–15]. The changes of size and shape in GQDs induce the modulation of their band-gap and optical response [9,14,15]. On the other hand, efficient edge functionalization mechanisms have been proposed in order to control the band-gap of these nanostructures [10]. All this has triggered the realization of many theoretical investigations that focus on studying of physical-chemical properties of GQDs [11,14,16–21]. The effects of the modification of the size and shape of GQDs were studied using the sp3 tight-binding (TB) model [14]. The results show that the energy band-gap decreases as the number of atoms in GQDs augments, independently of the particular shape. Other effective TB models for geometrically optimized GQDs show that changes in the geometry lead to the modification of the energy spectrum [19]. The effects of applied external electric field on the optical response of GQDs have also been studied employing the TB formalism. It is shown that the intensity of transition peaks changes drastically as a function of the electric field strength [18]. The tight-binding models are used as well to investigate the energy states of double triangular GQDs [16]. The structures are assumed to be horizontally coupled via several kinds of mechanisms. As a result, it is possible to conclude that different types of connections between the GQDs, have distinct effects on the energy levels spectrum in the system. The use of the TB scheme in triangular and hexagonal GQDs is presented in Ref. [22], and a similar treatment in the case of bilayer GQDs was put forward in Ref. [23].

Moreover, in a very recent report Eich et al. have put forward the possibility of fabricating devices with coupled active quantum dots in bilayer graphene [24]. Speaking about stacked graphene-based systems, another way to modulating the opto-electronic properties has to do with the stacking of vertical layers or nanostructures, with angular twisting between the stacking components. This kind of manipulation of the electronic properties of stacked structures has been named as twistronics [25]. For example, twisting in stacked graphene layers shows that the opto-electronic properties could be modulated by changing the amplitude of the twisting angle [25–29].

Motivated by the above-described situation, in this work we are aimed at studying the opto-electronic properties of twisted bilayer GQDs. For that purpose, we make use of a simple tight-binding approach that includes the interaction between the two monolayers of the GQD. The features of the energy spectrum and the related optical properties are then investigated, exploring the combined effects of GQD's shape, size and angle of twisting on the energy levels and the optical response. Our results show that the energy levels of coupled stacked GQDs are modulated by the twisting angle and, depending on the shape of the GQDs, it is found that the system can either have a semiconductor or a metallic behavior. These results set up a new possibility to control the opto-electronic properties of GQDs and may open a direction of prospective applications.

In this work we consider two different geometrical shapes of GQDs: triangle-like, otherwise named as triangular (TGQD); and circle-like circular, otherwise named as circular (CGQD), as shown in Fig. 1 . Clearly, due to the particular lattice structure of graphene, the mentioned geometry in each case is only approximated and our QD structures actually appear as inscribed within one of those two simple 2D forms; but we shall keep those nominations all throughout. In the case of TGQDs, we have taken into account two possible edge configurations: zigzag (Z-TGQD) and armchair (A-TGQD). The lateral size, L, in this case, ranks from 8.2 to 69.1 nm in the A-TGQD whilst it goes from 7.2 to 43.7 nm when dealing with Z-TGQD. These values ensure the conservation of the equilateral shape of the TGQDs. For the CGQDs we will have a combination of zigzag and armchair edges depending on the value of the CGQD radius. It is worth mentioning that circular QDs in graphene can be field-induced via the combination of an electrostatic field from a tip or a top gate electrode and a magnetic field, as commented in Ref. [30]. We assume that the same type of structures can be obtained in a twisted graphene bilayer as it has been assumed in the case of trilayer ones [31].

The twist between the two stacked TGQDs starts from the so-called AA configuration. For TGQDs we consider arbitrary angles of relative rotation. However, in the present case, for twisted CGQD we shall consider commensurable graphene layer twisting, as described below. This is in analogy with previous reports [32,33], where the Dirac equation is solved for bilayer biased circular dots within the continuous model with the inclusion of infinite mass border conditions. In that work it is considered that such edge states can be induced by the substrate and we assume that the same edge phenomenon can take place in rotated bilayers. Nonetheless, solutions via finite element methods, as carried out in Ref. [31] for a trilayer GQD structure, or by means of analytical expressions, as reported in Ref. [34] for a bilayer GQD system, can give the results for any value of the rotation angle. However, while the continuous model applies for any arbitrary value of the rotation angle, the obtained results are valid only in the low energy regime. Therefore, the entire spectrum of optical absorption would not be fully described. That is one of the reasons for maintaining the use of TB models and commensurable cell descriptions.

Within a general framework, in any commensurable rotation of a pair of Bravais lattices Moiré patterns can be studied [35]. Two-layer twisted Bravais structures such as centered and non centered squares, rectangles and hexagons can be seen as a so-called Bravais-Moire lattice. In the particular case of two graphene layers, the points are carbon atoms forming a honeycomb lattice with sides of length a 0 = 1.44 Å, the distance between nearest neighbor carbon atoms. Under a commensurable rotation, the symmetry of the resulting Moiré patterns divides the plane into six equal parts. So in terms of the non-rotated reference system, we can define six primitive vertices. The primitive vertices form a super hexagonal cell which is useful for studying some additional geometrical properties of commensurability and non-commensurability. For instance, the addressed Bravais-Moiré theory provides two types of super hexagonal cells: Type I, in which all the atoms are inside the primitive cell (extensively used in literature, see Ref. [36] and the references therein); or Type II, where there are also atoms on the sides of the hexagon. In the second case, two additional atoms appear trisecting each side of the super cell, and the well known formula for the number of atoms in a commensurable cell must be adjusted. There is a number of periodic structures defined by this super hexagonal cell: the usual parallelogram super cell and other structures defined by symmetries in the super hexagon, such as triangles, trapeziums, etc. In particular, inscribed and circumscribed circles are of interest because a new radial periodicity can be defined when the circles are concentric, i.e. when the angle parameters are not coprimes. In this work we study some concentric structures of commensurable hexagons inscribed in circles with different radius, defined by the primitive vertices. Accordingly, when dealing with commensurable angles associated with the circular dots in a graphene bilayer, they will be given by the relation cos
α =
m
2
+ 4 m n +
n
2
2
(
m
2
+ m n +
n
2
)
, and the circle radius will be in that case equal to
(
m 2
+ m n +
n 2
)
a
c c
. In accordance, CGQDs will be characterized by the pair of indices (m, n), -for details see Ref. [35]-. For twisted CGQDs we shall report on angles between 21.79° and 48.36°, and circular radii varying between 3.8 and 15.2 Å. These values are defined by different pairs (n, m).

In order to obtain the allowed energies of the structure we use the tight-binding model (TB), which is well described in Refs. [37–41]. In the case of graphene bilayers the TB Hamiltonian is
(1)
H =
H
1
+
H
2
+
H
12
,
where H 1 and H 2 are the individual QD Hamiltonians and H 12 represents the interaction betweeners the two layin the system. In the language of the second quantization the single layer Hamiltonian is written as
H
n
= − t
∑
< i j >
∣
ϕ
j
A
> <
ϕ
i
B
∣ + h . c . , where ∣
ϕ
i
A B
> is the standard wave function notation for carbon atom sites and t is the hopping parameter, which is here taken as −3.16 eV. The Hamiltonian that describes the interaction between the layers is given by
H
12
=
∑
i , j
γ
1
e
− β
(
r
→
i , j
−
d
→
)
|

j
A
> <
ϕ
i
B
| + h . c . . In this expression γ 1 = −0.39 eV is the interlayer hopping parameter. The vector
r
→
i , j
associates with the distance between atoms in different graphene layers;
d
→
is the vector that correspond to the interlayer distance and β is a dimensionless parameter with value 3.0. This model has been employed to study the optical absorption of twisted trilayer graphene systems, showing that the TB results are in agreement with the DFT calculations [42]. Accordingly, this TB model can represent correctly the interaction of graphene multilayered systems. In this work we have extended it to determine the energy spectrum of interacting graphene QDs.

With the information on the electronic structure of conduction (E
c ) and valence (E
v ) states it is possible to evaluate the imaginary part of the dielectric function, ɛ 2(ω), in the form
(2)
ɛ
2
(
ω
)
=
2 π e
m ω
2
∑
c , v
M
c , v
δ
E
c
−
E
v
− ℏ ω
where the indices c and v stand for the respective sets of quantum numbers corresponding to conduction and valence band energy levels, and M
c,v is the corresponding electric dipole matrix element. Then, the coefficient of interband optical absorption can be evaluated from Ref. [37]:
(3)
β =
ɛ
2
ω
n c
where c is the speed of light and n is the static value of the refractive index.

An equilateral triangular quantum dot (TGQD), should consist of an integer number of hexagonal elementary cells, therefore, depending on the side of the (armchair or zigzag), the triangles have different edge sizes, as mentioned. Figs. 2 and 3
present the calculated spectra of bilayer A-TGQDs and Z-TGQDs, respectively, as functions of dot size, for different angles of relative twisting. It is readily noticed -as expected-that, in both cases, the spectrum turns denser for larger dot size for both valence and conduction states. The bilayer A-TGQD system is a semiconducting one, with a wider band-gap for the smaller dot sizes, which is not strongly-affected by the relative rotation as observed from Fig. 6(a). There, one notices that the increase in the energy gap with the angle takes place within a smaller range of the angle -starting from zero-as long as the size of the dots goes bigger. Then the energy gap value remains almost constant or may even decrease. But a significant fact is that, for higher twisting angles, the size of the energy band gap of large bilayer QDs remains wide enough as compared with the α = 0° value. This indicates that relative rotation is a suitable tool for opening an energy band-gap in large A-TGQDs.

The situation with the bilayer Z-TGQDs is quite different. The unrotated system is, in all cases, a small-gap semiconductor with the energy band-gap slightly diminishing as the result of the increment of the dot size. The reason for this small-gap behavior relates with the energy states that localize immediately above and below the Fermi level (zero energy state). If it were not for the presence of these states, the structure would behave much similarly as the armchair one. In a single Z-TGQD structure degenerate zero-energy states appear due to the broken sublattice symmetry. The degree of degeneration is equal to the difference between the number of A and B atoms, and the states that belong to the degenerate shell localize mainly at the edge of the triangle [43]. Such degeneration is broken in the bilayer structure, leading to the appearance of HOMO and LUMO states on both sides of the zero energy level, as can be observed from Figs. 4 and 5
. There, the electronic densities corresponding to such states are represented for a Z-TGQD with 36 a 0 of side length. In the figures that correspond to the unrotated structure it can be seen that the edge-like character of the states is much less pronounced than in the case discussed in Ref. [43], due precisely to the degeneracy breaking associated to interlayer coupling. It is worth mentioning that the appearance of these edge-like states spread out in the gap energy region of bilayer Z-TGQD has been also previously reported in the presence of an electric field [23] which plays, in that case, an analogous -degeneracy-breaking- role to twisting.

On the other hand, when the two graphene QD layers are each other rotated the energy gap starts decreasing and zero-gap states occur for certain values of α, depending on the dot's size. These zero-gap states may appear for isolated rotation angle values, as noticed in the case of smaller QDs, or for continuous intervals of α, as it can be observed in the case of the QD with 358 atoms (not declared in the table above and shown here in order to better illustrate that feature). Again, going over the graphics in Figs. 4 and 5, one realizes that twisting causes the strengthening of the edge-like character of HOMO and LUMO states, which is the reason behind the appearance of the zero-gap states in the system.

The above described behaviors obey a main physical reason, related with the degree of inter-layer coupling. This is obviously modified by the twisting, taking into account the high directionality of the p
z orbitals. As noticed from Eq. (1), there is a twist-induced change in the layer atom coordinates. This means that the coupling between carbon atoms in different layers will be affected. A good example of this is the one depicted in Fig. 4(b) for the smallest Z-TGQD (5a 0 -13 atoms). It is possible to observe that as long as the α angle augments from zero to 30° the energy band-gap diminishes from 0.67 eV to zero. When α = 30° the two layers are positioned in such a way that no interlayer coupling is present (all atoms in the upper layer are shifted in such a way that no orbital finds a neighbor in the lower one to couple with). Indeed, this is a feature that occurs for situations involving small enough triangular QDs because for larger ones is less likely to have such a “disconnected” configuration. In the particular case discussed, the further increment in the twisting angle amplitude has the effect of restoring the initial structure when α = 60°. That is the reason for the symmetric behavior of the energy band-gap dependence.

A better picture of the above mentioned phenomenon can be presented by means of a detailed analysis of a particular case. In Fig. 7 we are presenting the case of a Z-TGQD-9 together with a representation of the relative array of the two layers for different angles of twisting. Each of these arrays corresponds to a value of the energy band-gap, as indicated in Fig. 7(a). It is readily observed that the maximum gap energy corresponds to the unrotated structure in which all atoms in the lower layer have its counterpart in the upper layer directly above, in such a way that the there is a full coupling on the two graphene sheets. The lowest value of the band-gap is associated with the configuration in which all carbon atoms are misaligned (α = 23°). Then, for a wider angle of 41°, there is a secondary maximum. The latter corresponds to misaligned geometry as well, but it is possible to see that there are some atoms in the unrotated layer which become almost aligned with their counterparts above, thus leading to a stronger coupling. Finally, a secondary minimum is observed when α = 60°, that is a symmetrical setup in which the coupling is weaker because in spite of the complete alignment of some of the atoms, the majority of them are misaligned in such a way that binding between the layers is negligible at those points. In fact, what we observe in the sequence of graphs (1)–(4) in Fig. 7 is a transition from two different kinds of regular stacking: AA [picture (1)] to AB [picture (4)], whilst graphs (2) and (3) represent intermediate configurations.

The variation of the imaginary part of the interband dielectric function with respect to the photon energy in the case of A-TGQDs appears in Fig. 8 . The results correspond to three distinct values of n for the dot size and three different angles of twisting. The results for x and y polarizations of the incident light are distinguished by the kind of plotting line used: solid for x polarization and dashed for y polarization. As can be observed, in general both kinds of variations lead to the overall redshift of the optical response, which is in complete accordance with the evolution of the spectrum depicted in Fig. 2. On the other hand, the change in the twisting angle (with triangle side length fixed) can result in the amplification or reduction of certain absorption peaks, attending to the symmetry and spatial dependence of the wavefunctions involved in the particular dipole matrix element. A better picture of this aspect can be obtained by observing Fig. 9 in which the amplitude of ɛ 2 is plotted as a function of both the photon energy and the angle of rotation, for distinct values of the A-TGQD size and two different in-plane linear polarizations of the incident light. For instance, one notices that as long as the triangle side dimensions augment the transitions associated to larger energy differences exhibit bigger amplitudes. Although one can identify amplitude and peak position differences between the two incident light polarizations in all cases, the main ones are noticed for the smaller dot sizes. For example, from Fig. 9(a and b) one observes that in such case y-polarization associates with larger amplitudes at bigger values of the rotation angle. This is something that can be related with the particular symmetry of the wavefunctions [18], provided the chosen armchair edge geometry is symmetrical if one selects -as we do-the y-axis to pass through any of the triangle vertices.

The imaginary part of the dielectric function in the case of Z-TGQDs is presented in Figs. 10 and 11
. It can be noticed that in the case of very small dot size, low-energy transitions are not present, starting with only a weak response [see indicating vertical arrows in Fig. 10(a)] that corresponds to transitions from the HOMO state to the lowest conduction level, for all angles of relative rotation considered. Low-energy transitions between the HOMO and LUMO states appear to be forbidden due to selection rules since the symmetries of both states may remain unaltered when the QD size is too small. As long as the dimensions of the bilayer QD increase, a transition peak of significant relative amplitude appears [see indicating oblique arrow in Fig. 10(c)] at low energies. It corresponds to HOMO to LUMO state transitions. This happens for both x- and y-polarization and, as noticed from Fig. 11. According to the results depicted there, the main contribution to the interband optical absorption in Z-TGQDs would come from this low-energy transition between edge-localized states.

The calculated imaginary part of the dielectric function for these specific geometric setups of C-GQDs is shown in Fig. 13 as a function of the incident photon energy. One may notice that the threshold of interband absorption exhibits a redshift as long as the radius of the QD augments, in accordance with the above commented result. In the case of commensurable structures [Fig. 13(a–c)] this feature is readily apparent and, besides that, for the largest dot radii the amplitude of the valence to conduction state threshold transition becomes significantly larger, in each case. To help understanding this phenomenon we choose to discus the (6, 1) case appearing in Fig. 13(d). The energy corresponding to the main absorption peak in this case is associated to the transition between the states immediately below and above the Fermi level (zero energy). Here, it is worth observing the corresponding graphics in Figs. 14 and 15
. These two figures contain the graphical representation of the electron probability density at each atom site for those otherwise named HOMO and LUMO states, respectively.

It is possible to notice that the distributions of electronic probability density in the (6, 1) HOMO and LUMO states are not equally symmetric with respect to the polarization axis. In consequence, a selection rule restricting the transition is absent and the associated electric dipole moment matrix element is significantly different from zero. This fact causes the enhancement of the absorption peak amplitude observed in the corresponding curve in Fig. 13(b) in the region of lowest transition energies. The same kind of analysis can explain the low-energy behavior of the optical response for other (m, n) configurations.

Speaking about the electronic probability density for HOMO and LUMO states it is worth noticing that they also have a rather strong edge-like character. However, for the circular geometry the degree of degeneracy of the unrotated states is smaller because so it is the difference between the numbers of atoms in sublattices A and B. Observing the graphics in Figs. 14 and 15, one may realize that HOMO and LUMO probability density distribution patterns seem to correspond to broken-degeneracy states only in the cases of larger radii.

We must emphasize that in our calculation we have avoided the analysis of spin effects which, particularly in the triangular geometry, appear due to the difference in the number of atoms remaining in each sublattice after the appropriate cutting of the graphene sheet is performed to obtain the quantum dot structure in each layer. However, we believe that the kind of method here employed constitutes a rather simple and convenient way to show the main qualitative features -with a fair quantitative description-of the electronic spectrum in this kind of systems as it was already reported in Ref. [42]. With its help, other structures of the same kind could also be investigated.

In this work we have investigated the properties of the electronic spectrum and interband optical response in stacked bilayer graphene quantum dots of triangular and circular geometries using the tight-binding method, taking into account the relative rotation of one layer with respect to the other. Triangular dots have been considered both in the armchair and zigzag border configurations whereas circular structures are chosen in such a way that dot size and angle of twisting comply with commensurability relations.

The energy spectrum of triangular bilayer quantum dots reveals their semiconducting character. Armchair quantum dots are wide-to-intermediate gap semiconductor systems, depending on the dot dimensions, with a slight increase in the optical band gap associated to smaller angles of rotation. Zigzag triangular quantum dots are narrow gap semiconductors with some zero-gap states appearing as a result of relative layer rotation. This feature has to do with the presence of edge-like HOMO and LUMO states symmetrically with respect to the Fermi level. These states are a consequence of the breaking of edge-like state degeneracy for the zero energy states of uncorrelated zigzag graphene quantum dots. On the other hand, the spectra of HOMO and LUMO states in circular bilayer graphene quantum dots also exhibit edge-like features, with a noticeable Bravais-Moiré pattern of the site distribution of electronic probability density in the case of larger dot sizes.

The interband optical response in these systems reveal a complicated pattern of allowed valence-to-conduction band transitions, with an overall redshift for both increasing dot size and twisting angle amplitude. Rather significant differences between x-polarization and y-polarization of the incident light are detected at higher twisting angles in the case of small size armchair triangular quantum dots.

Perhaps, the most prominent feature of the investigated optical properties is the low-energy response associated to transitions between the HOMO and LUMO states in zigzag triangular and in circular bilayer graphene quantum dots. The associated absorption peaks in these cases show the larger relative amplitudes. For the structures considered here, these transitions are in the order of several tens of THz. Thus, such kind of systems could become candidates for applications in optoelectronics in that frequency range.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Anton Tiutiunnyk expresses gratitude to Morelos Autonomous State University and Mexican PRODEP for a Postdoctoral grant 2016-2018. C. A. D. is grateful to the Colombian Agencies: CODI-Universidad de Antioquia (Estrategia de Sostenibilidad de la Universidad de Antioquia and projects “Propiedades magneto-ópticas y óptica no lineal en superredes de Grafeno” and “Estudio de propiedades ópticas en sistemas semiconductores de dimensiones nanoscópicas”), and Facultad de Ciencias Exactas y Naturales-Universidad de Antioquia (CAD exclusive dedication projects 2018–2019).
